title: Dog Temporary Blindness After Seizure: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Understand why dogs experience temporary blindness after seizures and learn when to seek veterinary care for postictal vision loss.
content_html:
TL;DR: Postictal blindness is a temporary loss of vision that occurs while a pet's brain recovers from a seizure and typically resolves within a few hours. While often a normal part of recovery, you should contact a veterinarian if the blindness lasts over 24 hours or if the seizure was prolonged or repetitive.

What is postictal blindness and why does it happen to my pet?

Temporary blindness following a seizure is a common symptom of the postictal phase, which is the recovery period for the brain after a seizure occurs. During a seizure, the brain experiences a massive surge of electrical activity. As the brain attempts to reset, it can cause transient neurological deficits, including vision loss. This happens because the visual processing centers in the brain are temporarily overwhelmed or exhausted and need time to return to normal function.

Is my pet's temporary blindness after a seizure a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for temporary blindness following a single seizure is categorized as Medium.
  • Postictal blindness is usually temporary and often resolves within minutes to a few hours.
  • Contact a veterinarian immediately if the seizure lasted longer than five minutes.
  • Seek immediate care if your dog has more than one seizure in 24 hours.
  • Consult a vet if the blindness persists for more than 24 hours.
  • A veterinary exam is always recommended after any first-time seizure event to determine the underlying cause.

How do photos and videos of my pet help the veterinarian?

  • Providing your veterinarian with a photo or video of your dog’s eyes and their behavior is an invaluable triage tool.
  • Visual evidence allows the vet to see the size of the pupils and how they react to light.
  • Video helps identify whether there are involuntary eye movements known as nystagmus.
  • This information helps the clinical team distinguish between a neurological recovery phase and other medical emergencies, such as a primary eye injury or severe brain swelling.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following a seizure in dogs, temporary blindness can occur due to intracranial disease affecting the optic chiasm, optic tracts, or occipital cortex, resulting in central blindness with normal pupillary light reflexes. One potential cause is hepatic encephalopathy, often seen in breeds like Miniature Schnauzers and Yorkshire Terriers due to congenital portosystemic shunts, leading to cerebral dysfunction. Cortical blindness with absent menace response, but normal pupillary light reflex may also indicate Polioencephalomalacia, where animals may separate from the group, stop eating, and display twitches of the ears and face.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: How long does temporary blindness usually last after a seizure?
faqs.answer: Most dogs regain their sight within a few minutes to several hours, though in some cases, the postictal phase can last up to 24 hours.

faqs.question: Should I try to test my dog's vision at home?
faqs.answer: You can gently move your hand toward your dog's eye to see if they blink (menace response), but be careful as disoriented dogs may accidentally bite if startled.

faqs.question: How can I keep my dog safe while they cannot see?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog in a small, carpeted room or a padded crate to prevent them from falling down stairs or bumping into sharp furniture corners.
